author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

ccel.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

source.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

title: Homily VI

source.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This Homily is intended to shew that the fear of Magistrates is beneficial.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It also contains an account of what occurred, during their journey, to those who were conveying the tidings of the sedition to the Emperor.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The case of Jonah is further cited in illustration.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The exhortation on the fear of death is here continued; and it is shewn, that he who suffers unjustly, and yet gives thanks to God, by whose permission it happens, is as one suffering for God’s sake.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Examples are again adduced from the history of the Three Children, and the Babylonian furnace.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The Homily concludes with an address on the necessity of abstaining from oaths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We have spent many days addressing words of comfort to your Charity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We would not, however, on that account lay the subject aside; but as long as the sore of despondency remains, we will apply to it the medicine of consolation.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if in the case of bodily wounds, physicians do not give over their fomentations, until they perceive that the pain has subsided; much less ought this to be done in regard to the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Despondency is a sore of the soul; and we must therefore foment it continually with soothing words.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For not so naturally is warm water efficacious to soften a hard tumour of the flesh, as words of comfort are powerful to allay the swelling passions of the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Here, there is no need of the sponge as with physician, but instead of this we employ the tongue.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: No need of fire here, that we may warm the water; but instead of fire, we make use of the grace of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Suffer us then to do so to-day.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if we were not to comfort you, where else could ye obtain consolation?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The judges affright; the priests therefore must console!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The rulers threaten; therefore must the Church give comfort!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus it happens with respect to little children.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The teachers frighten them, and send them away weeping to their mothers; but the mothers receiving them back to their own bosoms, keep them there, embrace them, and kiss them, while they wipe away their tears, and relieve their sorrowing spirits; persuading them by what they say, that it is profitable for them to fear their teachers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Since therefore the rulers also make you afraid, and render you anxious, the Church, which is the common mother of us all, opening her bosom, and cradling us in her arms, administers daily consolation; telling us that the fear of rulers is profitable, and profitable too the consolation that comes from hence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the fear of the former does not permit us to be relaxed by listlessness, but the consolation of the latter does not allow us to sink under the weight of sadness; and by both these means God provides for our safety.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He Himself hath armed magistrates with power; that they may strike terror into the licentious; and hath ordained His priests that they may administer consolation to those that are in sorrow.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And both these things are taught us by the Scripture, and by actual experience of recent events.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if, whilst there are magistrates and soldiers living under arms, the madness of a few individuals, a motley crew of adventurers, hath kindled such a fire among us, in so short a moment of time, and raised such a tempest, and made us all to stand in fear of shipwreck, suppose the fear of magistrates to be wholly taken away?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: To what lengths would they not have gone in their madness?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Would they not have overthrown the city from its foundations, turning all things upside down, and have taken our very lives?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If you were to abolish the public tribunals, you would abolish all order from our life.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And even as if you deprive the ship of its pilot, you sink the vessel; or as, if you remove the general from the army, you place the soldiers bound in the hands of the enemy; so if you deprive the city of its rulers, we must lead a life less rational than that of the brutes, biting and devouring one another; the rich man, the poorer; the stronger man, the weaker; and the bolder man, him who is more gentle.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But now by the grace of God none of these things happen.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For they who live in a state of piety, require no correction on the part of the magistrates; for “the law is not made for a righteous man,” saith one.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But the more numerous being viciously inclined, if they had no fear of these hanging over them, would fill the cities with innumerable evils; which Paul knowing, observed, “There is no power, but of God, the powers that be are ordained of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For what the tie-beams are in houses, that rulers are in cities; and in the same manner as if you were to take away the former, the walls, being disunited, would fall in upon one another of their own accord; so were you to deprive the world of magistrates, and of the fear that comes of them, houses at once, and cities, and nations, would fall on one another in unrestrained confusion, there being no one to repress, or repel, or persuade them to be peaceful, by the fear of punishment!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us not then be grieved, beloved, by the fear of our rulers, but let us give thanks to God that He hath removed our listlessness, and rendered us more diligent.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For tell me, what harm hath arisen from this concern and anxiety?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Is it that we are become more grave, and gentle; more diligent, and attentive? that we see no one intoxicated, and singing lascivious airs?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Or is it that there are continual supplications, and prayers, and tears? that unseasonable laughter, and impure words, and all dissoluteness is banished; and that the city is now in all respects, like the pattern of a modest and virtuous woman?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Dost thou grieve, I ask, for any of these reasons?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For these things, assuredly, it were right to rejoice, and to be thankful to God, that by the terror of a few days He hath put an end to such stupidity!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Very true,” saith some one, “if our danger did not go beyond fear, we should have reaped a sufficient benefit; but we are now in dread lest the mischief should proceed much farther, and we should be all placed in the extremest peril.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nevertheless, I say, fear not.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Paul comforteth you, saying, “God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able, but will with the temptation also make the way of escape, that ye may be able to bear it.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He indeed Himself hath said, “I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For had He resolved to punish us in deed, and in actual endurance, He would not have given us over to terror during so many days.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when He would not punish, He affrights; since if He were intending to punish, fear would be superfluous, and threatening superfluous.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But now, we have sustained a life more grievous than countless deaths; fearing and trembling during so many days, and being suspicious of our very shadows; and paying the punishment of Cain; and in the midst of our sleep, starting up, through constant agony of mind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So that if we have kindled God’s wrath, we have appeased Him in the endurance of such a punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if we have not paid the satisfaction due to our sins, yet it hath been enough to satisfy the mercy of God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But not this, but many other grounds for confidence ought we to have.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For God hath already given us not a few pledges for favourable hopes.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And first of all, those who carried the evil tidings departing hence with the speed of wings, supposing they should long ere this have reached the camp, are yet delayed in the midst of their journey.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So many hindrances and impediments have arisen; and they have left their horses, and are now proceeding in vehicles; whence their arrival must of necessity be retarded.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For since God here stirred up our priest, and common father, and persuaded him to go forth, and undertake this embassy, he detained the messengers for a while, when they were but half way on their road, lest arriving before him they might kindle the fire, and make our teacher’s efforts to mend matters useless, when the royal ears had become inflamed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For that this hindrance on the road, was not without God’s interposition is evident from this.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Men who had been familiar with such journeys all their lives, and whose constant business it was to ride on horseback, now broke down through the fatigue of this very riding; so that what hath now happened is the reverse of what took place in the case of Jonah.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For God hastened him when unwilling, to go on his mission.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But these, who were desirous to go, He hindered.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: O strange and wonderful event!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He wished not to preach of an overthrow; and God forced him to go against his will.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These men with much haste set forward to be the bearers of a message of overthrow, and against their will again He has hindered them!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For what reason think you?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Why, because in this case the haste was an injury; but in the other case, haste brought gain.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: On this account, He hastened him forward by means of the whale; and detained these by means of their horses.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou the wisdom of God?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Through the very means by which each party hoped to accomplish their object, through these each received an hindrance.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Jonah expected to escape by the ship, and the ship became his chain.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These couriers, by means of their horses, expected the more quickly to see the Emperor; and the horses became the obstacles; or rather, neither the horses in one case, nor the ship in the other, but the Providence of God everywhere directing all things according to its own wisdom!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Consider also His care over us, and how He both affrighted and consoled us.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For after permitting them to set out on the very day when all these outrages were committed, as if they would report all that had taken place to the Emperor; He alarmed us all at their sudden departure.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But when they were gone, and two or three days had elapsed, and we thought the journey of our Priest would now be useless, as he would arrive when it was too late, He delivered us from this fear, and comforted us by detaining them, as I observed, midway; and by providing persons coming to us from thence by the same road, to announce to us all the difficulties they had met with on their journey, that we might thus take a little breath, as indeed we did, and were relieved of a great part of our anxiety.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Having heard of this, we adored God who had done it, who hath even now more tenderly than any father disposed all things for us, delaying by some invisible power those evil messengers, and all but saying to them, “Why do ye hasten?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Why do ye press on, when ye are going to overwhelm so great a city?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For are ye the bearers of a good message to the Emperor?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Wait there till I have made ready my servant, as an excellent physician, to come up with you and anticipate you in your course.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if there was so much of providential care in the first breaking out of this wound of iniquity, much more shall we obtain a greater freedom from anxiety, after conversion, after repentance, after so much fear, after tears and prayers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For Jonah was very properly constrained, in order that he might be forcibly brought to repentance; but ye have already given striking evidences of repentance, and conversion.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore, it is necessary that you should receive consolation, instead of a threatening messenger.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason also hath He sent our common father hence, notwithstanding the many things to hinder it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if He had not been tender of our safety, He would not have persuaded him to this, but would have hindered him, however disposed he might be to undertake the journey.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: There is a third reason by which I may possibly persuade you to have confidence; I mean, the present sacred season, which almost all, even unbelievers, respect; but to which this our divinely-favoured Emperor has shewn such reverence and honour, as to surpass all the Emperors who have reigned with a regard for religion before him.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As a proof of this, by sending a letter on these days in honour of the feast, he liberated nearly all those who were lodged in prison; and this letter our Priest when he arrives will read to him; and remind him of his own laws, and will say to him, “Do thou exhort thyself, and remember thine own deeds!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast an example for thy philanthropy at home!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou didst choose to forbear from executing a justifiable slaughter, and wilt thou endure to perpetrate one that is unjust.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Reverencing the feast, thou didst discharge those who had been convicted and condemned; and wilt thou, I ask, condemn the innocent, and those who have not committed any violence, and this when the sacred season is present?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: That be far from thee, O Emperor!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou, speaking by this Epistle to all the cities, didst say, ‘Would it were possible for me to raise even the dead.’

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This philanthropy and these words we now stand in need of.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: To conquer enemies, doth not render kings so illustrious, as to conquer wrath and anger; for in the former case, the success is due to arms and soldiers; but here the trophy is simply thine own, and thou hast no one to divide with thee the glory of thy moral wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou hast overcome barbarian war, overcome also Imperial wrath!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let all unbelievers learn that the fear of Christ is able to bridle every kind of authority.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Glorify thy Lord by forgiving the trespasses of thy fellow-servants; that He also may glorify thee the more; that at the Day of Judgment, He may bend on thee an Eye merciful and serene, being mindful of this thy lovingkindness!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This, and much more, he will say, and will assuredly rescue us from the Emperor’s wrath.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And not only will this fast be of the greatest assistance to us in influencing the Emperor in our favour, but also towards enduring what befalls us with fortitude; for we reap no small consolation from this season.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For our very meeting together daily as we do, and having the benefit of hearing the divine Scriptures; and beholding each other; and weeping with each other; and praying, and receiving Benedictions, and so departing home, takes off the chief part of our distress.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us, therefore, not despond, nor give ourselves up by reason of our distress; but let us wait, expecting a favourable issue; and let us give heed to the things that are now about to be spoken.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it is my purpose to discourse to you again to day respecting contempt for death.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I said to you, yesterday, that we are afraid of death, not because he is really formidable; but because the love of the kingdom hath not kindled us, nor the fear of hell laid hold of us; and because besides this we have not a good conscience.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Are you desirous that I should speak of a fourth reason for this unseasonable distress, one which is not less, and truer than the rest?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We do not live with the austerity that becometh Christians.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: On the contrary, we love to follow this voluptuous and dissolute and indolent life; therefore also it is but natural that we cleave to present things; since if we spent this life in fastings, vigils, and poverty of diet, cutting off all our extravagant desires; setting a restraint upon our pleasures; undergoing the toils of virtue; keeping the body under like Paul, and bringing it into subjection; not “making provision for the lusts of the flesh;” and pursuing the strait and narrow way, we should soon be earnestly desirous of future things, and eager to be delivered from our present labours.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And to prove that what I say is not untrue, ascend to the tops of the mountains, and observe the monks who are there; some in sackcloth; some in bonds; some in fastings; some shut up in darkness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou wilt then perceive, that all these are earnestly desiring death, and calling it rest.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For even as the pugilist is eager to leave the stadium, in order that he may be freed from wounds; and the wrestler longs for the theatre to break up, that he may be released from his toils; so also he who by the aid of virtue leads a life of austerity, and mortification, earnestly longs for death in order that he may be freed from his present labours, and may be able to have full assurance in regard to the crowns laid up in store, by arriving in the still harbour, and migrating to the place where there is no further apprehension of shipwreck.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore, also, hath God provided for us a life that is naturally laborious and troublesome; to the end that being here urged by tribulation, we may conceive an eager longing for future blessings; for if now, whilst there are so many sorrows, and dangers, and fears, and anxieties, surrounding us on all sides, we thus cling to the present life; when should we ever be desirous of the life to come, if our present existence were altogether void of grief and misery?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus also God acted towards the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For wishing to infuse into them a desire of returning (to Canaan), and to persuade them to hate Egypt, He permitted them to be distressed by working in clay, and brick-making, that being oppressed by that weight of toil and affliction, they might cry unto God respecting their return.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if, indeed when they departed after these things had happened, they did again remember Egypt, with their hard slavery, and were urgent to turn back to that former tyranny; what if they had received no such treatment from these barbarians? when would they have ever wished to leave that strange land?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: To the end, therefore, that we may not be too closely attached to the earth, and grow wretched whilst gaping after present things, and become unmindful of futurity, God hath made our lives here full of labour.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us not then cherish the love of the present life beyond what is necessary.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For what doth it profit us? or what is the advantage of being closely rivetted to the desire of this present state?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Art thou willing to learn in what respect this life is advantageous?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is so, inasmuch as it is the ground-work and starting point of the life to come; the wrestling-school and the arena for crowns of victory hereafter! so that if it does not provide these for us, it is worse than a thousand deaths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if we do not wish to live so as to please God, it is better to die.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For what is the gain?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What have we the more?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do we not every day see the same sun, and the same moon, the same winter, the same summer, the same course of things?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “The thing that hath been, shall be; and that which is done, is that which shall be done.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us not then at once pronounce those happy, who are alive, and bewail the dead, but let us weep for those who are in their sins, whether they be dead or alive.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And on the other hand, let us call those happy in whatsoever condition they be, who are in a state of righteousness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou, forsooth, fearest and lamentest “one” death; but Paul, who was dying daily, was so far from shedding a tear on that account, that he rejoiced and exulted!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “O that I did endure the peril for God,” saith some one, “then I should have no anxiety!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But do not even now sink into despondency; for not only indeed is he well approved, who suffers in the cause of God: but he who is suffering any thing unjustly: and bearing it nobly, and giving thanks to God who permits it, is not inferior to him who sustains these trials for God’s sake.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The blessed Job is a proof of this, who received so many intolerable wounds through the devil’s plotting against him uselessly, vainly, and without cause.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Yet, nevertheless, because he bore them courageously, and gave thanks to God who permitted them, he was invested with a perfect crown.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Be not sad then on account of death; for it is natural to die: but grieve for sin; because it is a fault of the will.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if thou grievest for the dead, mourn also for those who are born into the world; for as the one thing is of nature, so is the other too of nature.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Should any one, therefore, threaten thee with death, say to him, “I am instructed by Christ not to ‘fear them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul.’”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Or should he threaten thee with the confiscation of thy goods, say to him, “Naked came I out of my mother’s womb, and naked shall I return thither.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We brought nothing into this world, and it is certain we can carry nothing out.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “And though thou take me not, death will come and take me; and though thou slay me not, yet the law of nature will presently interfere and bring the end.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore we should fear none of these things which are brought on us by the order of nature, but those which are engendered by our own evil will; for these bring forth our penalty.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But let us continually consider this, that as regards the events which come upon us unexpectedly we shall not mend them by grieving, and so we shall cease to grieve.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And moreover we should think of this again, that if we suffer any evil unjustly, during the present life, we discharge a multitude of sins.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore it is a great advantage to have out the chastisement of our sins here, and not there; for the rich man received no evil here, and therefore he was scorched in the flames there; and that this was the reason why he did not enjoy any consolation, hear in proof what Abraham saith, “Son, thou hast received thy good things; therefore thou art tormented.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But that to the good things bestowed on Lazarus, not only his virtue, but his having here suffered a thousand ills, contributed, learn also from the patriarch’s words.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For having said to the rich man, “Thou hast received thy good things,” he goes on to say, “and Lazarus evil things, and for this reason he is comforted.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For as they who live virtuously, and are afflicted, receive a double reward from God, so he who liveth in wickedness, and fares sumptuously, shall have a double punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Again, I declare this not for the purpose of accusing those who have taken flight, for it is said, “Add not more trouble to a heart that is vexed;” nor do I say it because I wish to rebuke; (for the sick man stands in need of consolation); but for the purpose of endeavouring to promote an amendment.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us not entrust our safety to flight, but flee from sins, and depart from our evil way.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If we escape from these things, although we be in the midst of ten thousand soldiers; not one of them will be able to smite us; but not flying from these, though we ascend to the very summit of the mountains, we shall there find innumerable enemies!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us again call to mind those three children, who were in the midst of the furnace, yet suffered no evil, and those who cast them into it, how they that sat around were all consumed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What is more wonderful than this?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The fire freed those it held possession of, and violently seized those whom it did not hold, to teach thee, that not the habitation, but the habit of life, bringeth safety or punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Those within the furnace escaped, but those without were consumed.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: To each alike were the same bodies, but not the same dispositions.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason neither were the effects on them the same; for hay, although it lie without the flame, is quickly kindled; but gold, although it remain within, becomes the more resplendent!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Where now are those who said, “Let the Emperor take all, and grant us our bodies free?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let such go and learn what is a free body.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is not immunity from punishment that makes the body free, but perseverance in a life of righteousness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The bodies of these youths, for instance, were free, though they were given over to the furnace, because they had before put off the slavery of sin.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this alone is liberty; and not an immunity from punishment, or from suffering anything fearful.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But having heard of the furnace, call thou to mind the “rivers of fire,” which there shall be in that fearful day.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For as on the above occasion, the fire seized upon some, but reverenced others, so also shall it be with those rivers.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If any one should then have hay, wood, stubble, he increases the fire; but if he has gold and silver, he becomes the brighter.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us therefore get together this kind of material, and let us bear the present state of things nobly; knowing that this tribulation will both bring us deliverance from that punishment if we understand how to practise true wisdom, and will also make us better here; and not only us, but often those too, who throw us into trouble, if we be vigilant; so abundant is the force of this spiritual wisdom; which was the case then even with the tyrant.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when he knew that they had suffered no harm, hear how he changed his language.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Ye servants of the most high God, come forth, and come hither.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Didst not thou say, a little before “Who is that God that shall deliver you out of my hands?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What hath happened?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whence this change?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou sawest those without destroyed, and dost thou call on those within?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whence hath it come to pass that thou art grown wise in such matters.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thou seest how great a change took place in the monarch!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whilst he had not yet exercised his power over them, he blasphemed, but as soon as he had cast them into fire, he began to shew moral wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason also God permitted all to take place, whatsoever the tyrant wished, in order that He might make it manifest, that none will be able to injure those who are kept by Him.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And what He did towards Job, He performed here.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For on that occasion also, He permitted the devil to manifest all his power; and not till he had exhausted all his darts, and no further mode of plotting against him remained, was the combatant led out of the field, that the victory might be brilliant and indubitable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So here too He did the very same thing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He willed to overthrow their city, and God stayed him not: he willed to carry them away captive, and He hindered him not: he willed to bind them, and He permitted; to cast them into the furnace, and He allowed it: to heat the flame beyond its measure, and this too He suffered; and when there was nothing further left for the tyrant to do, and he had exhausted all his strength, then God manifested His own power, and the patience of the youths.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou how God permitted these tribulations even to the end, that He might shew the assailants the spiritual wisdom of those whom they assailed, as well as His own providence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Both of which circumstances also that man then discerned, and cried out, “Ye servants of the most high God, come forth, and come hither.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But consider thou with me the magnanimity of the youths; for they neither sprang out before the call, lest some should suppose they feared the fire; nor when they were called did they remain within, lest any one should think that they were ambitious and contentious.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “As soon,” say they, “as thou hast learnt whose servants we are, as soon as thou hast acknowledged our Lord, we come forth to be heralds to all who are present of the power of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Or rather, not only they themselves, but even the enemy with his own voice, yea, both orally, and by his epistle, proclaimed to all men both the constancy of the combatants, and the strength of Him who presided over the contest.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And even as the heralds, when they proclaim the names of the victorious combatants in the midst of the theatre, mention also the cities to which they belong; “such an one, of such a city!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So he too, instead of their city, proclaimed their Lord, by saying, “Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, ye servants of the most high God, come forth, and come hither.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What is come to pass, that thou callest them the servants of God?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Were they not thy servants?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Yea,” saith he, “but they have overthrown my sovereignty; they have trampled under foot my pride.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They have shown by deeds, that He is their true Lord.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If they were the servants of men, the fire would not have feared them; the flame would not have made way for them; for the creation knows nothing of reverencing or honoring the servants of men.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore again he saith, “Blessed be the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Contemplate with me also, how first he proclaims the Arbiter of the contest.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Blessed be God, who hath sent His angel and delivered His servants.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This of the power of God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He speaks also of the virtue of the combatants.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “Because they trusted in Him, and have changed the king’s word, and have yielded their bodies, that they might not worship any god except their own God.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Could anything equal the virtue of this?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Before this, when they said, “We will not serve thy gods,” he was inflamed more fiercely than the very furnace; but now, when by their deeds they had taught him this, he was so far from being indignant, that he praised and admired them, for not having obeyed him!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So good a thing is virtue, that it has even its enemies themselves to applaud and admire it!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These had fought and conquered, but the vanquished party gave thanks, that the sight of the fire had not terrified them, but that the hope in their Lord had comforted them.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And He names the God of the whole world after the three youths, not at all circumscribing His sovereignty, but inasmuch as these three youths were equivalent to the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason he both applauds those who had despised him, and passing by so many governors, kings, and princes, those who had obeyed him, he stands in admiration of the three captives and slaves, who derided his tyranny!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For they did these things, not for the sake of contention, but for the love of wisdom; not of defiance, but of devotion; not as being puffed up with pride, but fired with zeal.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For great indeed is the blessing of a hope in God; which then also the barbarian learned, and making it manifest that it was from that source they had escaped the impending peril, he exclaimed aloud: “Because they trusted in Him!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But I say all this now, and select all the histories that contain trials and tribulations, and the wrath of kings, and their evil designs, in order that we may fear nothing, save only offending God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For then also was there a furnace burning; yet they derided it, but feared sin.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For they knew that if they were consumed in the fire, they should suffer nothing that was to be dreaded; but that if they were guilty of impiety, they should undergo the extremes of misery.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is the greatest punishment to commit sin, though we may remain unpunished; as on the other hand, it is the greatest honour and repose to live virtuously, though we may be punished.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For sins separate us from God; as He Himself speaks; “Have not your sins separated between you and Me?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But punishments lead us back to God.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As one saith, “Give peace; for Thou hast recompensed us for all things.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Suppose any one hath a wound; which is the most deserving of fear, gangrene, or the surgeon’s knife? the steel, or the devouring progress of the ulcer?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Sin is a gangrene, punishment is the surgeon’s knife.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As then, he who hath a gangrene, although he is not lanced, hath to sustain the malady, and is then in the worse condition, when he is not lanced; so also the sinner, though he be not punished, is the most wretched of men; and is then especially wretched, when he hath no punishment, and is suffering no distress.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And as those who have a disease of the spleen, or a dropsy, when they enjoy a plentiful table, and cool drinks, and a variety of delicacies, and condiments, are then especially in a most pitiable state, increasing as they do their disease by luxury; but should they rigorously subject themselves to hunger and thirst, according to medical laws, they might have some hope of recovery; so also those who live in iniquity, if they are punished, may have favourable hopes; but if, together with their wickedness, they enjoy security and luxury, they become more wretched than those who cram their bellies, though they are in a state of dropsy; and so much the more, as the soul is better than the body.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If then thou seest any who are in the same sins, and some of them struggling continually with hunger, and a thousand ills; while others are drinking their fill, and living sumptuously, and gormandizing; think those the better off, who endure sufferings.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For not only is the flame of voluptuousness cut off by these misfortunes, but they also depart to the future Judgment, and that dread tribunal, with no small relief; and go hence, having discharged here the penalty of the greater part of their sins by the ills they have suffered.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But enough of consolation.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is time for us now, at last, to proceed to the exhortation on the subject of avoiding oaths, and to remove that seeming palliation on behalf of those who swear, which is but futile, and useless.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when we bring an accusation against them, they allege the case of others who do the very same thing; and they say, “such and such persons swear.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us then say to these, Nevertheless; such a man does not swear: and God will give His judgment concerning thee, from those who do good works; for sinners do not profit sinners by fellowship in transgressions; but they who perform what is right condemn sinners.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For they who gave not Christ food, or drink, were many; but they rendered no aid to each other.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Similar also was the case of the five virgins, who found no pardon from companionship, but being condemned by a comparison with those who had acted wisely, both these and the former were alike punished.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Dismissing then this argument of frigid self-deception, let us not look at the case of those who fall, but at those who fashion their conduct rightly; and let us endeavour to carry along with us a memento of the present fast when it is over.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And as it often happens when we have purchased a vestment, or a slave, or a precious vase, we recall again the time when we did so, and say to each other, “That slave I purchased at such a festival; that garment I bought at such a time;” so, in like manner, if we now reduce to practice this law, we shall say, I reformed the practice of swearing during that Lent; for till then I was a swearer; but from barely hearing an admonition, I have abstained from the sin.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But “the custom,” it may be objected, “is a hard thing to be reformed.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I know it is; and therefore am urgent to throw you into another custom, which is good and profitable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when you say, it is difficult for me to abstain from what is habitual; for that very reason, I say, you should make haste to abstain, knowing for certain, that if you once make another custom for yourself of not swearing, you will want no labour afterwards.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Which is the more difficult thing; not to swear, or to remain the whole day without food; and to shrivel up on water-drinking, and meagre diet?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is evident that the latter surpasses the former; yet, notwithstanding, custom has made this matter so possible and easy of execution, that when the fast comes round, although any one should exhort a thousand times, or as frequently constrain and compel one to partake of wine, or taste of any other of those things which are forbidden during fasts, yet a man would prefer to suffer anything, rather than touch the prohibited article of food; and that not for want of relish for the table, nevertheless, we bear it all with fortitude, from the habit of our conscience.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And the case will be the same in regard to oaths; and just as if now, any one were to impose ever so great necessity, you would remain immovable, holding fast the habit; so also in that case, if any one should urge you ten thousand times, you would not depart from your custom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When you go home, therefore, discourse of all these things with those who are in your house; and as many persons often do, when they come back from a meadow, having plucked there a rose, or a violet, or some flower of that kind, they return twisting it about with their fingers; and as some, again, when they quit the gardens to go home, take with them branches of trees, with their fruit upon them; and as others, moreover, from sumptuous feasts, carry away leavings of the entertainment for their dependents; so indeed do thou, departing from hence, take an exhortation home to thy wife, thy children, and all thine household.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this admonition is more profitable than the meadow, the garden, or the banquetting table.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These roses never wither; these fruits never drop off; these dainties never corrupt.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The former yield a temporary delight; but the latter a lasting advantage, not only after this reformation has taken place, but in the very act of reforming.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For think what a good practice this would be, having dismissed all other matters public or private, to discourse only of the divine laws continually, at the table, in the forum, and in your other meetings.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Would we give our attention to these things, we should say nothing of a dangerous or injurious nature, nor should we sin unwittingly.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Giving our leisure to discourse respecting these things, we should be able to withdraw our soul even from this despondency that hangs over us, instead of looking with so much anxiety as we do, whilst we say one to another, “Hath the Emperor heard what hath happened?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Is he incensed?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What sentence hath he pronounced?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hath any one petitioned him?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Will he himself endure to destroy utterly a city so great and populous?”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Casting these and all such cares upon God, let us be anxious only as to what He hath commanded!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Thus shall we rid ourselves of all these sorrows; and although ten only among us should succeed, the ten would quickly become twenty; the twenty fifty; the fifty a hundred; the hundred a thousand; the thousand all the city.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And just as when ten lamps are lighted, one may easily fill the whole house with light, so also with respect to right actions; should only ten act rightly, we shall light up a general flame throughout the city, to shine forth, and to procure us safety.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For not so naturally does the fire, when it falls upon a forest, kindle the neighbouring trees successively, as will the emulation for virtue, when it seizes upon a few minds, be mighty in its progress to diffuse itself through the whole community.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Give me cause, then, to exult over you both in the present life, and at that future Day, when those to whom talents have been entrusted, shall be summoned!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Your good reputation is a sufficient reward for my labours; and if I see you living in piety, I have all I wish.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do, then, what yesterday I recommended, and to-day will repeat, and will not cease to say it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Fix a penalty for those who swear; a penalty which is a gain, and not a loss; and prepare yourselves henceforth so as you may give us a proof of success.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For I shall endeavour to hold a long conversation with each of you, when this assembly is dismissed; in order that in the continuance of discourse I may discover the persons who have been acting rightly, and those who have not.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And if I find any one still swearing, I shall make him manifest to all who are amended, that by reproving, rebuking, and correcting, we may quickly deliver him from this evil habit.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For better it is that he should amend through being reproached here, than that he should be put to shame, and punished, in the presence of the whole assembled universe, on that Day, when our sins shall be revealed to the eyes of all men!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But God forbid that any in this fair assembly should appear there suffering such things! but by the prayers of the holy fathers, correcting all our offences, and having shown forth the abundant fruit of virtue, may we depart hence with much confidence, through the grace and lovingkindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom, and with whom, be glory to the Father together with the Holy Spirit, for ever and ever.
